---
title: "Chapter 20 — 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 19 · 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning](ch19.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [21 · 🌫️ Diffusion Models →](ch21.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Generative models learn the underlying distribution of data so they can *create* new examples — faces that never existed, plausible sentences, synthetic molecules. Where a classifier asks "what label fits this input?", a generative model asks "what does data like this look like, and can I produce more?" They sit at the heart of modern deep learning, powering image synthesis, data augmentation, anomaly detection, and the representation learning that underpins much of generative AI.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep learning / unsupervised generation · used to synthesize realistic data and learn latent structure · the one key idea — model the full data distribution $p(x)$, then sample from it.
:::

## 20.1 — Generative vs discriminative: two ways to learn

The cleanest way to understand generative models is to contrast them with the classifiers that dominate supervised learning. A **discriminative model** learns the conditional distribution $p(y \mid x)$ — given an input $x$, how probable is each label $y$? It draws decision boundaries and never needs to know what the inputs themselves look like. Logistic regression and most neural classifiers are discriminative.

A **generative model** instead learns $p(x)$ (or the joint $p(x, y)$). It models how the data itself is distributed, which means it can *generate* new samples by drawing from that distribution. This is a strictly harder problem: capturing the full structure of "what a face looks like" is far more demanding than separating cats from dogs.

The intuition: a discriminative model memorizes the fence between two pastures; a generative model learns to draw the sheep. Knowing how to draw a sheep implicitly tells you which pasture it belongs in (you can classify via Bayes' rule, $p(y\mid x) \propto p(x\mid y)\,p(y)$), but the reverse is not true — knowing the fence tells you nothing about how to draw a sheep.

The core difficulty is that for rich data, $p(x)$ has no closed form. A $256\times256$ RGB image lives in a space of nearly 200,000 dimensions, and real images occupy a vanishingly thin, curved sliver of it — the **data manifold**. The three classical deep families — GANs, VAEs, and normalizing flows — are three different bets on how to tame that intractable distribution. They differ mainly in *how they treat the density* $p(x)$: skip it, bound it, or compute it exactly.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Want to model data x] --> B{How to handle p of x?}
    B -->|Skip it, learn to sample| C[GAN: adversarial, implicit density]
    B -->|Bound it, learn latent z| D[VAE: variational, approximate density]
    B -->|Compute it exactly| E[Flow: invertible, exact density]
```

::: {.callout-tip}
A useful rule of thumb: if you only ever need *samples*, an implicit model (GAN) can suffice; if you need a *number* — a likelihood or density — you need a VAE or a flow.
:::

## 20.2 — Generative Adversarial Networks: the forger and the detective

The GAN idea is a game between two networks. A **generator** $G$ takes random noise $z \sim p(z)$ (say a standard Gaussian) and maps it to a fake sample $G(z)$. A **discriminator** $D$ takes a sample and outputs the probability that it is real rather than generated. Picture a forger painting fake banknotes and a detective trying to spot them: as the detective sharpens, the forger must improve, and at equilibrium the forgeries are indistinguishable from the real thing.

Formally this is a **minimax game** with a single value function:

$$\min_G \max_D \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

The discriminator maximizes this (it wants $D(x)\to 1$ on real data and $D(G(z))\to 0$ on fakes); the generator minimizes it (it wants $D(G(z))\to 1$). At the theoretical optimum, $G$ reproduces the data distribution and $D$ outputs $1/2$ everywhere — it can no longer tell real from fake. One can show that, for a fixed optimal discriminator, the generator is effectively minimizing the **Jensen–Shannon divergence** between the real and generated distributions.

The crucial property is that the generator never sees real data directly and $p(x)$ is never written down. The model is **implicit**: it defines a distribution only through the sampling procedure $z \mapsto G(z)$. That is why GANs cannot give you a likelihood, but it is also why they can produce extraordinarily sharp samples — they are optimized purely to fool a learned critic, not to spread probability mass to cover everything.

```python
# One GAN training step (the mechanism, not a framework call)
# z: noise batch, x_real: real batch; G, D are networks
def gan_step(G, D, x_real, z, opt_D, opt_G):
    # --- train discriminator: push real->1, fake->0 ---
    x_fake = G(z).detach()                  # stop gradient to G here
    loss_D = -(log(D(x_real)) + log(1 - D(x_fake))).mean()
    opt_D.zero_grad(); loss_D.backward(); opt_D.step()

    # --- train generator: make D call fakes real ---
    x_fake = G(z)
    loss_G = -log(D(x_fake)).mean()         # "non-saturating" form
    opt_G.zero_grad(); loss_G.backward(); opt_G.step()
```

Note the `loss_G` above uses $-\log D(G(z))$ rather than the original $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. This **non-saturating** trick fixes a practical flaw: early in training the discriminator easily rejects fakes, so $\log(1-D(G(z)))$ has near-zero gradient and the generator cannot learn. Flipping to maximize $\log D(G(z))$ gives strong gradients exactly when the generator is doing badly.

## 20.3 — Why GANs are hard: mode collapse and instability

GANs are famous for being temperamental, and the reasons are structural, not just engineering hiccups.

**Mode collapse** is the signature failure. The generator discovers one (or a few) outputs that reliably fool the current discriminator and collapses onto producing only those — generating, say, only one style of digit instead of all ten. The minimax objective does not actually penalize this directly: a generator that outputs a single perfect fake still wins the round. Diversity is only enforced indirectly, through the discriminator eventually learning to recognize the over-produced mode and the two networks endlessly chasing each other between modes.

**Training instability** comes from the fact that two networks are chasing a moving target rather than descending a fixed loss. There is no single objective whose value going down means "things are improving" — $G$ and $D$ improve at each other's expense, and the dynamics can oscillate, diverge, or stall. If the discriminator becomes too strong, its gradients to the generator vanish (the Jensen–Shannon divergence saturates when the two distributions barely overlap); if too weak, it provides no useful signal.

Common stabilizers, in roughly historical order:

- **DCGAN** — replaced fully-connected layers with convolutions, added batch normalization, and prescribed architectural rules that made image GANs trainable at all.
- **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN / WGAN-GP)** — replaced the Jensen–Shannon objective with an approximation of the **Earth Mover's distance** (how much "work" it takes to morph one distribution into the other), giving smooth, non-vanishing gradients even when real and fake distributions barely overlap; the gradient penalty enforces the Lipschitz constraint the distance requires.
- **Spectral normalization** — caps the discriminator's Lipschitz constant by normalizing weight matrices by their largest singular value, a cheap and widely used regularizer.

::: {.callout-warning}
A falling generator loss does **not** mean a GAN is improving. Because $G$ and $D$ are adversaries, losses are relative — both can stay flat while sample quality climbs, or $G$'s loss can drop precisely because $D$ collapsed. Judge GANs by samples and metrics like FID, never by the loss curve alone.
:::

## 20.4 — Measuring generative quality: FID and friends

If you cannot trust the loss curve, how do you tell a good generative model from a bad one? The honest answer is that evaluating generation is genuinely hard — there is no single label to check against — so the field relies on a handful of proxy metrics, each capturing a different failure mode.

The dominant one is the **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)**. The intuition: pass a large batch of real images and a large batch of generated images through a fixed, pretrained image network (Inception), grab the feature vectors from a deep layer, and ask "do these two clouds of features look like they came from the same distribution?" FID models each cloud as a Gaussian and measures the distance between them:

$$\text{FID} = \lVert \mu_r - \mu_g \rVert^2 + \operatorname{Tr}\!\big(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\big)$$

where $(\mu_r, \Sigma_r)$ and $(\mu_g, \Sigma_g)$ are the mean and covariance of the real and generated feature sets. **Lower is better** — zero means the two feature distributions coincide. Crucially, FID rewards both *fidelity* (sharp, realistic samples pull $\mu_g$ toward $\mu_r$) and *diversity* (mode collapse shrinks $\Sigma_g$ and inflates the score), which is exactly why it caught on as the default for image GANs.

A few other metrics fill gaps:

| Metric | What it measures | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Inception Score (IS) | Sample sharpness + class diversity, from a classifier's confidence | higher better |
| FID | Distance between real/generated feature distributions | lower better |
| Precision / Recall | Fidelity (precision) vs. coverage (recall), split apart | higher better |
| Negative log-likelihood | Held-out density, for models that give one (VAE bound, flow exact) | lower better |

The split into **precision and recall** is worth knowing: precision asks "are the samples realistic?" and recall asks "do they cover the full variety of real data?". A mode-collapsed GAN can score high precision yet terrible recall — a single distinction FID blends into one number.

::: {.callout-warning}
FID is not an absolute score. It depends on the feature network, the number of samples, and image preprocessing, so FID values are only comparable when computed the exact same way. Treat it as a *relative* ranking tool, not a physical constant.
:::

## 20.5 — The GAN zoo: DCGAN, conditional GANs, StyleGAN

Once the basic recipe stabilized, architectural variants pushed GANs to photorealism and control.

**Conditional GANs (cGANs)** add a condition $c$ — a class label, a text embedding, or an input image — to *both* networks, so the generator models $p(x \mid c)$. This turns the unconditional "draw any face" into "draw a face wearing glasses" or, in image-to-image work like pix2pix, "turn this sketch into a photo." The condition is simply concatenated into the generator's input and the discriminator's input, and the discriminator must now judge whether a sample is real *and* consistent with $c$.

**StyleGAN** redesigned the generator to give controllable, high-resolution synthesis. Instead of feeding noise straight in at the bottom, it first maps $z$ through an MLP to an intermediate **latent space** $w$, then injects $w$ at every resolution layer via adaptive instance normalization. This separates coarse attributes (pose, face shape) from fine ones (hair texture, freckles), enabling style mixing and smooth interpolation. StyleGAN-family models produced the famous "this person does not exist" faces.

| Variant | Key change | What it buys you |
|---|---|---|
| DCGAN | Conv generator + batchnorm | Stable image training |
| cGAN / pix2pix | Condition $c$ in $G$ and $D$ | Controllable / paired translation |
| WGAN-GP | Wasserstein loss + grad penalty | Far fewer collapse failures |
| StyleGAN | Mapping net + per-layer style injection | High-res, disentangled control |

The throughline: GANs give the best raw sample sharpness of the classical families, at the cost of no likelihood and the most finicky training.

## 20.6 — Variational Autoencoders: probabilistic latent space

A VAE takes the opposite bet from a GAN: embrace probability, accept slightly blurrier samples, and get a principled, stable objective with a usable latent space.

Start with the picture. A VAE assumes each data point $x$ was generated by first drawing a low-dimensional **latent code** $z$ from a simple prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, then passing it through a **decoder** $p_\theta(x \mid z)$ that paints the data. To generate, you sample $z$ from the prior and decode. The problem is learning: to train, you need the posterior $p(z \mid x)$ — "which codes could have produced this image?" — and that is intractable, because it requires the evidence $p(x) = \int p_\theta(x\mid z)\,p(z)\,dz$, an integral over every possible latent code.

The VAE's move is to introduce an **encoder** $q_\phi(z \mid x)$, a network that approximates the true posterior by outputting a Gaussian's mean and variance for each input. This is the **variational inference** idea: replace an intractable distribution with the closest tractable one. Now you can train both networks together by maximizing a tractable lower bound on the data likelihood, the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**:

$$\log p_\theta(x) \;\ge\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{reconstruction}} \;-\; \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}\!\big(q_\phi(z\mid x)\,\|\,p(z)\big)}_{\text{regularizer}}$$

Read the two terms as a tug-of-war. The **reconstruction** term wants the decoder to rebuild $x$ accurately from its code — it pulls the encoding to be informative. The **KL term** (a **Kullback–Leibler divergence**, measuring how far one distribution sits from another) measures how far the encoder's per-example distribution drifts from the prior $\mathcal{N}(0,I)$, and pulls every code back toward a clean, standard-normal blob. Balancing them produces a latent space that is both faithful and smooth enough to sample from. The "lower bound" is exact up to the gap $D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z\mid x)\,\|\,p(z\mid x))$ — how well the encoder approximates the true posterior — so maximizing the ELBO does double duty: it fits the data *and* sharpens the encoder. (The $\beta$-VAE simply scales the KL term by a factor $\beta$ to trade reconstruction quality against disentanglement of the latent factors.)

## 20.7 — The reparameterization trick and sampling

There is a snag hiding in the ELBO. The reconstruction term is an expectation over $z \sim q_\phi(z\mid x)$, and we want gradients to flow back into the encoder parameters $\phi$. But you cannot backpropagate through a random sampling operation — "draw a sample" has no derivative.

The **reparameterization trick** dissolves the problem by moving the randomness outside the network. Instead of sampling $z$ directly from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, you sample a fixed noise variable $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and compute

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon.$$

Now $z$ is a deterministic, differentiable function of the encoder outputs $\mu$ and $\sigma$, with all the randomness sequestered in $\epsilon$, which has no parameters. Gradients pass cleanly through $\mu$ and $\sigma$.

```python
# VAE forward pass + loss (mechanism only)
def vae_loss(x, encoder, decoder):
    mu, logvar = encoder(x)                  # per-example Gaussian params
    std = exp(0.5 * logvar)
    eps = randn_like(std)                    # noise lives outside the net
    z = mu + std * eps                       # reparameterization trick

    x_hat = decoder(z)
    recon = mse(x_hat, x)                    # reconstruction term
    # closed-form KL between N(mu,sigma^2) and N(0,I):
    kl = -0.5 * sum(1 + logvar - mu**2 - exp(logvar))
    return recon + kl                        # negative ELBO, to minimize
```

Once trained, generation ignores the encoder entirely: draw $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, run the decoder, done. Because the KL term forced encodings to fill the prior smoothly, nearby codes decode to similar images, so you can interpolate between two samples and walk through a continuous space of plausible data — a property GANs achieve only with extra effort. The price is the well-known VAE blurriness: the Gaussian likelihood and the averaging in the ELBO push the decoder toward "safe," slightly smeared reconstructions rather than crisp details.

::: {.callout-tip}
The reparameterization trick is the single idea that makes VAEs trainable end-to-end. The pattern — push noise to a parameter-free input so the rest stays differentiable — recurs throughout stochastic deep learning.
:::

## 20.8 — Normalizing flows: exact likelihood by invertible maps

GANs give no likelihood; VAEs give only a lower bound. **Normalizing flows** are the family that computes $p(x)$ *exactly*. The price is a strict architectural constraint.

The intuition: take a simple distribution you fully understand — a Gaussian blob $z \sim p(z)$ — and push it through an **invertible** transformation $x = f(z)$ that warps the blob into the complicated shape of your data. Because $f$ is invertible, you can run it backwards: given any data point $x$, recover the exact $z = f^{-1}(x)$ that produced it. Think of squeezing and stretching a lump of dough — you rearrange where the mass sits, but you can always knead it back.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 140" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <circle cx="60" cy="70" r="34" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="60" y="74" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">z ~ N(0,I)</text>
  <path d="M110 70 H250" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="180" y="60" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">x = f(z)</text>
  <path d="M250 90 H110" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3" fill="none" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="180" y="108" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">z = f⁻¹(x)</text>
  <path d="M280 40 q40 0 28 30 q-12 30 20 30 q24 0 16 -28" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="312" y="120" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">p(x)</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 z" fill="currentColor"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>

The exact density comes from the **change-of-variables** formula:

$$\log p_x(x) = \log p_z\big(f^{-1}(x)\big) + \log \left| \det \frac{\partial f^{-1}}{\partial x} \right|$$

In words: the probability of $x$ equals the probability of its preimage $z$ under the simple prior, corrected by how much the transformation stretches or squeezes volume locally — the **Jacobian determinant**. (If $f$ expands a region, probability density there must thin out to keep the total mass at one; the determinant is the bookkeeping for that.) You can train a flow by directly maximizing this exact log-likelihood, no adversary and no bound.

Two hard requirements follow, and they shape every flow architecture:

- $f$ must be **invertible**, so you can map both directions.
- The **Jacobian determinant** must be cheap to compute — a general $D \times D$ determinant costs $O(D^3)$, which is hopeless for images.

The trick that makes flows practical is the **coupling layer** (RealNVP): split the input in half, leave the first half untouched, and transform the second half using parameters computed from the first. The Jacobian of this operation is triangular, so its determinant is just the product of the diagonal — linear time. Stack many such layers (permuting which half is fixed) and you get an expressive yet exactly-invertible map.

```python
# RealNVP coupling layer: invertible, with a cheap (triangular) Jacobian
def coupling_forward(x, net):
    x1, x2 = split(x)                 # x1 passes through unchanged
    s, t = net(x1)                    # scale & shift depend only on x1
    y2 = x2 * exp(s) + t             # affine transform of x2
    y = concat(x1, y2)
    log_det = sum(s)                 # det of triangular Jacobian = prod of diag
    return y, log_det

def coupling_inverse(y, net):
    y1, y2 = split(y)
    s, t = net(y1)                   # same net, x1 == y1 recovers s, t exactly
    x2 = (y2 - t) * exp(-s)         # exact inverse
    return concat(y1, x2)
```

Flows give exact likelihoods and a true, invertible latent space, which makes them excellent for density estimation and anomaly scoring. Their weakness is efficiency and capacity: the invertibility constraint forbids the dimensionality reduction VAEs enjoy (latent and data dimensions must match) and forces many layers to reach the expressiveness GANs get more cheaply.

## 20.9 — Choosing a family: the generative trilemma

There is a recurring "generative trilemma": high sample quality, fast sampling, and mode coverage / exact likelihood are hard to get all at once. Each classical family picks two and sacrifices the third.

| | GAN | VAE | Normalizing flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density $p(x)$ | None (implicit) | Lower bound (ELBO) | Exact |
| Sample quality | Highest (sharp) | Lower (blurry) | Moderate |
| Sampling speed | Fast (one pass) | Fast (one pass) | Fast (one pass) |
| Training stability | Poor (adversarial) | Good | Good |
| Latent space | Yes, no encoder by default | Yes, smooth, with encoder | Yes, exactly invertible |
| Mode coverage | Weak (collapse risk) | Good | Good |

Read the table as a decision guide. Need the sharpest possible images and can tolerate finicky training? GAN. Need a smooth latent space, stable training, and a probabilistic encoder for downstream tasks? VAE. Need an *exact* likelihood for density estimation or anomaly detection? Flow. These families are also routinely combined — VAE-GAN hybrids use an adversarial loss to sharpen a VAE's blurry decoder, and flows are sometimes used as expressive VAE priors.

The trilemma is also why **diffusion models** took over much of generative AI: they achieve GAN-level sample quality with VAE-level training stability and good mode coverage, paying instead in slow, iterative sampling. They are covered in their own chapter.

::: {.callout-warning}
Do not compare these families by a single "best samples" leaderboard. A flow with worse-looking samples may be the right tool because it gives an exact likelihood a GAN simply cannot produce. Match the model to whether you need samples, a density, or a usable latent space.
:::

## 20.x — Key takeaways

- **Generative models learn $p(x)$** and can sample new data; discriminative models learn only $p(y\mid x)$ — generation is the harder problem, because real data occupies a thin manifold in a huge space.
- **GANs** play a minimax game between a generator and discriminator (implicitly minimizing Jensen–Shannon divergence): sharpest samples, no likelihood, prone to **mode collapse** and unstable training. DCGAN, WGAN-GP, cGAN, and StyleGAN are the key stabilizers and extensions.
- **VAEs** maximize the **ELBO** (reconstruction − KL), use the **reparameterization trick** to stay differentiable, and yield a smooth, samplable latent space — at the cost of blurrier outputs.
- **Normalizing flows** use invertible maps and the change-of-variables formula to compute **exact likelihoods**, constrained to cheap-Jacobian, invertible architectures like coupling layers.
- **Evaluate** generative models with proxy metrics like **FID** (lower is better; rewards fidelity *and* diversity) and precision/recall — never by the training loss alone.
- The **trilemma** — sample quality vs. exact likelihood vs. training stability — explains why each family makes different trade-offs, and why diffusion models (separate chapter) became dominant.

## 20.y — See also

- **Chapter 19 — Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning** (the deterministic autoencoder that the VAE makes probabilistic)
- **Chapter 21 — Diffusion Models** (the family that resolves much of the generative trilemma)
- **Chapter 14 — Neural Network Fundamentals** and **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks** (the building blocks and optimization these models rely on)
- **Chapter 16 — Convolutional Neural Networks** (the backbone of image GANs and VAEs)
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** (variational inference and the KL divergence behind the ELBO)
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** (latent spaces and the manifold view of data)
- **Chapter 34 — Multimodal AI** (text-conditioned generation that builds on conditional generative models)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 19 · 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning](ch19.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [21 · 🌫️ Diffusion Models →](ch21.html)
